name: Bobby Troup
description: American actor, jazz pianist, singer and songwriter (1918-1999)
abstract: Robert William Troup Jr. (October 18, 1918 – February 7, 1999) was an American actor, jazz pianist, singer, and songwriter.

works_created.t: Their Hearts Were Full of Spring

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Wilder
about_quotes.evidence: He was my Captain... It was a while. When I first went in, I was in special weapons... It was Bobby Troup who began putting pressure... So they transferred me finally to the band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Wilder — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jack Sheldon
about_quotes.evidence: I was working with Julie London and Bobby Troup... We were working at Mr. Kelly’s in Chicago... And we started doing it with them.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack Sheldon interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 15, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Holman Bill
about_quotes.evidence: Stars of Jazz was a t.v. show. I guess Bobby was the host for a while. I‟m just trying to figure out if that could have been a t.v. show or a recording date. I really can‟t say.
about_quotes.doc_title: Holman Bill — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Wilder Part 2
about_quotes.evidence: I was in a unit that was being trained for small weapons... Bobby Troup... said that you might as well add him to the band. And I was transferred after boot camp to the band
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Wilder Part 2 interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, October 12, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Bushkin
about_quotes.evidence: of all guys I meet another Captain in the Marine Corps, was the guy that wrote “Route 66.” ... Bobby Troup, right. And he was in charge of the black troops
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Bushkin interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 15, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: JJ Johnson
about_quotes.evidence: I called him recently to tell him that we were going to record one of his little-known compositions... He was very pleased.
about_quotes.doc_title: JJ Johnson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
